content: All 5 portfolio companies use the same L1-L5 analytical hierarchy: L1 Size and Composition, L2 Cash Conversion, L3 Credit Quality, L4 Loss Attribution, L5 Forward Signals. This hierarchy drives tab organization, metric placement, methodology structure, and onboarding checklists. Every new metric must map to exactly one level.

HHI (Herfindahl-Hirschman Index) threshold of 0.15 indicates moderate concentration across all asset classes. HHI > 0.25 is high concentration requiring IC attention. Applied uniformly: Klaim by healthcare provider Group, SILQ by merchant, Tamara by product/geography, Aajil by customer. HHI time series trend (increasing/stable/decreasing) is as important as the absolute level.

PAR30 > 5% is the fund-level watchlist threshold. This applies across all portfolio companies regardless of asset class. PAR30 breaching 5% triggers a watchlist discussion at IC level. Note: the denominator matters - Tape Analytics uses lifetime PAR (vs total originated) as headline, with active PAR as context. Portfolio Analytics uses eligible outstanding for covenant monitoring.

Write-off definitions differ fundamentally by asset class and must never be compared directly across companies. Klaim: denial > 50% of purchase value (insurance risk). SILQ: DPD > 90 days (consumer credit risk). Aajil: all 19 write-offs are Bullet-type deals with 0 EMI write-offs (counterparty credit risk, structural). Ejari: 32 excluded loans in the original analysis (tenant payment risk). Tamara: DPD-based default buckets per HSBC covenant definitions.

Different asset classes have fundamentally different risk drivers that determine which analytical tabs and metrics are most important. Insurance risk (Klaim): denial rates by insurer, claim processing timelines, provider concentration. Consumer credit risk (SILQ, Tamara): DPD migration, vintage performance, underwriting drift. Counterparty credit risk (Aajil): customer concentration, deal type (EMI vs Bullet), industry exposure. Tenant payment risk (Ejari): Najiz legal recovery, seasonal patterns, segment analysis. The L1-L5 hierarchy is universal but the emphasis within each level is asset-class-specific.
